sentence: Subjective:
The patient, Frank, is a 65-year-old male who presents with a 1-week history of shortness of breath.
He reports that this has been ongoing for approximately 6 months, with a similar episode occurring 6 months ago, which improved after adjusting his Lasix dose.
He has a history of coronary disease, including a heart attack in 2018 with a stent placement in the right coronary artery, and a low ejection fraction of 35%.
He has been taking Lasix 40mg daily and reports no change in urination.
He denies any fever or recent illness.
He has experienced swelling in his legs and reports urinating at least once a night.
He has a history of knee pain and has taken Motrin in the past, but has been advised to avoid it due to kidney concerns.
Review of systems:
The patient reports shortness of breath with walking, particularly on stairs, and has had to use two pillows to sleep at night to alleviate symptoms.
He denies waking up short of breath.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of coronary disease, including a heart attack in 2018 with a stent placement in the right coronary artery, and a low ejection fraction of 35%.
He also has chronic kidney disease, stage 3.
Current medications:
Lasix 40mg daily
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: 135/80
Jugular venous distention: 9cm
Carotid bruits: none
Lung exam: bibasilar crackles with occasional rhonchi that clears with cough
Heart exam: 3/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left base
Lower extremity exam: 1-2+ pitting edema bilaterally
Test Results:
[NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient is experiencing an acute heart failure exacerbation; Coronary artery disease; Stage 3 chronic kidney disease; Possible gout flare.
Plan:
I prescribed Lasix 80mg daily to be taken in the morning, with a possible second dose in the afternoon if necessary.
I ordered a chest X-ray to rule out any other underlying conditions.
I also ordered an echocardiogram to assess the heart murmur and cardiac function.
I referred the patient to a dietitian to address his dietary needs, particularly with regards to salt intake.
I instructed the patient to avoid taking Motrin and Advil due to kidney concerns and to use Tylenol instead.
I also ordered a complete metabolic panel to assess kidney function.
Follow-up:
The patient will return in 2 days to report on his weight and any changes in symptoms.
If he experiences worsening shortness of breath, he will be admitted to the hospital for intravenous diuretics.
